entry.title: SAP Public Cloud Partner Advisory Council-Sales: Driving Better Collaboration and Product Usability
entry.content:
SAP Public Cloud Partner Advisory Council
Making collaboration more direct. Making products more usable.

As more customers adopt SAP Public Cloud, partners play an increasingly critical role in sales, delivery, and customer success.

At the same time, we have consistently heard feedback from our partners:

  • The need for earlier visibility into SAP’s roadmap
  • Better clarity on what capabilities will be delivered and when
  • A more direct way to bring real project requirements into product discussions

To address these needs, SAP Public Cloud Sales has launched the SAP Public Cloud Partner Advisory Council (PAC).

Why PAC?

Traditionally, partner feedback has been collected through channels such as WCR and CIP.

While these mechanisms are important for structured feedback, we have observed some limitations:

  • Feedback cycles can be relatively long, making it difficult to respond quickly to real project needs
  • Inputs are often based on individual requirements rather than broader business scenarios
  • The process of how feedback influences the product roadmap is not always transparent

PAC is designed to complement these channels by providing a more direct and interactive way to collaborate.

Our goal is simple: enable faster communication, clearer alignment, and improved product usability.

What we do in PAC

PAC focuses on real product topics and real project challenges.

Roadmap sharing
We share the latest roadmap updates for SAP Public Cloud (e.g. S/4HANA Cloud, BTP), including planned and under-evaluation capabilities, and explain their impact on sales and project delivery.

Real-time discussion and Q&A
We collect partner questions in advance, discuss key topics during the session, and define clear follow-up actions where needed.

Direct connection with product teams
PAC enables direct interaction between partners and product teams, helping ensure that real business scenarios are better reflected in product design.

Real use cases and experience sharing
Partners are encouraged to share project experiences, integration and extensibility scenarios, as well as sales and delivery insights.

What we have discussed so far

PAC sessions are built around real agendas.

Topics from previous sessions include:

  • SAP Cloud ERP for Sales strategy and roadmap
  • Fiori roadmap (e.g. 2602 / 2608) with system demos
  • Manage Sales Documents scenarios
  • Extensibility and UI use cases
  • Custom Sales Order Type
  • Partner Success Stories and Customer Stories
  • Product surveys, user research, and partner feedback

Each session also includes dedicated time for open discussion, Q&A, and clear next steps.

A continuously evolving format

PAC is not a one-time event.

We continuously shape future PAC sessions based on the topics partners are most interested in. This ensures that discussions remain relevant and that feedback can effectively influence product improvements.

What partners gain

By participating in PAC, partners can:

  • Better understand product direction and roadmap
  • Reduce uncertainty in sales and project delivery
  • Bring requirements more directly into product discussions
  • Learn from other partners’ real-world experiences

How to join

The SAP Public Cloud Partner Advisory Council is invitation-based.

To ensure open and productive discussions, participating partners are required to sign an SAP FBA (Feedback Agreement).

If you are interested in joining PAC, please contact:

Bernie.xiong@sap.com

We will follow up with further details and invitations.

entry.title: SAP Developer News for March 26th, 2026
entry.content:
DESCRIPTION

Bye Bye Build Apps

  • Deprecation Announcement (blog post): https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-build-apps-deprecation-and-the-path-forward/ba-p/14351750
  • The Path Forward, or what you can do now (documentation): https://help.sap.com/docs/build-apps/service-guide/sap-build-apps-path-forward?locale=en-US&q=sap+build+apps

Edge Integration Cell updates

  • What's new in Edge Integration Cell: https://help.sap.com/whats-new/cf0cb2cb149647329b5d02aa96303f56?locale=en-US&lastSent=2026-03-23&Component=Edge+Integration+Cell

A2A Editor

  • A2A Editor: https://open-resource-discovery.github.io/a2a-editor/
  • CodeJam Roadshow Brazil: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam-blog-posts/sap-codejam-roadshow-2026-brazil-edition-kicking-off-in-march-and/ba-p/14317926

DYK  #9 – Orchestration Client Configuration

  • More information: https://help.sap.com/docs/ai-launchpad/sap-ai-launchpad/orchestration-4953dc10c6dd48fe85f37b41109dffe2?q=orchestration+workflow+ai

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro

0:10 Build Apps Retirement

2:42 Edge Integration Cell updates

3:31 A2A Editor

4:58 DYK #9 Orchestration client Configuration

Transcription

00;00;00;17 - 00;00;06;15
Announcer Guy
This is the SAP Developer News for March, the 26, 2026.

00;00;06;18 - 00;00;09;26
Daniel Wroblewski
I’m recording this in the bomb shelter of our apartment building to give

00;00;09;26 - 00;00;38;06
Daniel Wroblewski
you some bad news. SAP Build Apps has been deprecated. Now, it all started in 2021 when SAP acquired AppGyver and over the next 12 to 18 months, it was integrated into BTP, most notably to support destinations so applications could access SAP data. In May of 2022, we started our 100 Days of Low code series.

00;00;38;11 - 00;01;11;22
Daniel Wroblewski
We had a challenge to see what the community, what types of apps the community members would create from SAP Build Apps. And in November of that year, we had the opportunity to share the stage at TechEd, the first post Covid in person TechEd, and we shared it with the CTO to announce SAP Build. And so as a AppGyver became one of the tools of that Low-Code suite and its name was changed to SAP Build Apps.

00;01;11;24 - 00;01;43;16
Daniel Wroblewski
But alas, in the age of AI with SAP Build Apps was supplanted by other tools. My colleague Patrick Scheid has created a blog post that explains the decision to deprecate, build apps and makes four key points. First of all, all development. Now all app development. No can be done with SAP Build and the tools that are available there, for example, Build Code and to create CAP applications.

00;01;43;18 - 00;02;23;05
Daniel Wroblewski
But SAP Build Apps will continue to be supported for those who have purchased it through the end of the contract. Now, existing web or mobile apps that you created with SAP Build Apps, unfortunately cannot be migrated to other tools, and you will need to recreate those apps from scratch using Build Code or any other development tool. Now to migrate back end, entities that you created with visual cloud Functions, you can export the data to CSV files and then import it to, other backends.

00;02;23;08 - 00;02;42;26
Daniel Wroblewski
But you will have to create those backends and those entities in order to import is it's been a long, strange trip with SAP AppGyver. And then as they Build Apps and I'm happy to have met and helped so many of you on their journey.

00;02;42;28 - 00;03;10;17
Antonio Maradiaga
Hola! SAP Developers. Edge Integration Cell version 8.40 is now available and it's packed with features you've been asking for. First, the NFS adapter, sender, and receiver. You can now connect directly to a network file system service or FTP2 adapter for all your EDI and B2B file transfers. So if you're doing all that file transfers, with your trading partners, this is what you need.

00;03;10;19 - 00;03;33;10
Antonio Maradiaga
Edge Integration Cell also now supports an external cloud connector. You no longer need to use the cloud connector that's running in the cluster. Finally, Edge Integration Cell also supports bulk, meaning that you can now use it as a data store. All the documentation links are in the description below. Hasta luego!

00;03;33;12 - 00;03;54;05
Nora von Thenen
As SAP is part of the A2A protocol initiative. My colleague Pavel Conniff just released a really cool new tool to contribute to the A2A ecosystem. The tool is called the A2A editor, and he developed it as an open source project funded by the EU. You can think of the A2A editor as a swagger like interface, but for designing agent cards and testing and validating the agent's capabilities.

00;03;54;07 - 00;04;11;10
Nora von Thenen
So I have part of the playground, which is part of the A2A editor. You have a JSON editor, an agent card overview, a chat integration to test an Http inspector, and more. So make sure you check it out under the URL somewhere here and in the show notes. I actually just tried it out and I find it really cool.

00;04;11;13 - 00;04;30;18
Nora von Thenen
I'm probably going to include it into the AI agent, the code base agent, CodeJam that Kevin and I just recently developed and started to give a couple of weeks ago in Amsterdam, and now we will also be doing a couple of those CodeJams in Brazil as part of the AI Roadshow. Vitaly I don't know, do we call it that?

00;04;30;20 - 00;04;59;29
Nora von Thenen
If not, we start calling it that now. So in the middle of April, we're actually going to Brazil for a lot of CodeJams all around the topic of AI. There will be HANA machine learning, getting started with, AI core, and also the new code based AI agents CodeJam. And in April or something else is happening around AI, which is the developer challenge on the topic of AI, but more to the next week.

00;05;00;04 - 00;05;26;13
Kevin Kiedelsheimer
Did you know that you can use the orchestration client from the SAP Cloud SDK for AI? To create a full AI orchestration, flow in CAP. You can simply instantiate an orchestration client from the orchestration package within the SDK, and then define the configuration for your orchestration flow via JavaScript object Notation. The orchestration client allows you to use different foundation models without the need to change the client code.

00;05;26;15 - 00;05;40;17
Kevin Kiedelsheimer
It allows you to configure templating, content filtering, data masking, and much more. You can either write the configuration directly or download it from the SAP AI launchpad and use it from file, or directly copy it into your project.

entry.title: Centralized Error Handling in SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP Cloud Integration, runtime errors can occur due to various reasons such as script failures, adapter issues, or unexpected payloads. Handling such errors individually in every integration flow often leads to duplicated logic and increased maintenance effort.

This blog demonstrates a centralized error handling mechanism in SAP Cloud Integration that:

  • Captures errors using an Exception Subprocess
  • Forwards error data using the ProcessDirect adapter
  • Sends HTML-formatted email notifications
  • Is reusable across multiple business integration flows

Solution

The solution consists of two types of integration flows:

1. Business Integration Flows

  • Executes business logic
  • Uses an Exception Subprocess to capture errors
  • Sends error details to a centralized logger

2. Central Error Logger Integration Flows

  • Receives error messages from multiple iFlows
  • Normalizes error data
  • Sends styled HTML email notifications to the support team

Step 1: Create the Business Integration Flow

1. Navigate to the SAP Integration Suite

2. Go to Design → Integrations and APIs

3. Create a new package (example: PCK_CentralErrorLogging)

4. Create a new Integration Flow named Business_Test_Iflow

This iFlow represents a sample business process where a runtime error occurs.

 

Step 2: Configure the Sender and Main Process

1. Add an HTTPS Sender Adapter

  • Configure endpoint address (example: /testError)
  • Configure authentication as required

2. Connect the adapter to the Start Event

3. Add a Groovy Script named Script_Groovy_Generate_Error

  • This script deliberately throws an exception for testing purposes

4. Connect the script to the End Event

 

Step 3: Add Exception Subprocess for Error Capture

1. Add an Exception Subprocess to the Business iFlow

2. Add a Groovy Script inside the subprocess named Script_Groovy_CaptureError

The Groovy script performs the following responsibilities:

  • Capture integration flow name
  • Capture message ID
  • Generate correlation ID
  • Capture error message
  • Generate timestamps (IST and UTC)

All values are stored as message properties. Using an Exception Subprocess enables centralized error capture without modifying individual processing steps.

 

Step 4: Send Error Details via ProcessDirect Adapter

1. After the Groovy script, add a ProcessDirect Receiver Adapter

2. Configure the address as /centralErrorLogger

This enables reusability across multiple business iFlows.

Step 5: Create the Central Error Logger Integration Flow

1. Create an integration flow named Central_Error_Logger_Iflow

2. Configure the Sender Adapter as ProcessDirect

3. Use the same address /centralErrorLogger

Step 6: Normalize Incoming Error Data

1. Add a Groovy Script as the first step

2. The script reads error details from properties, headers, or payload and applies fallback values if data is missing, preparing a consistent structure for email formatting.

This makes the central logger reusable.

Step 7: Format Error Details into HTML

1. Add a Content Modifier

2. Create an HTML table displaying:

  • Integration Flow Name
  • Message ID
  • Adapter Type
  • Error Message
  • Timestamp (IST and UTC)
  • Correlation ID

 

Step 8: Configure Email Notification

1. Add a Mail Receiver Adapter

2. Configure mail server, sender and receiver email IDs, subject, and set MIME type to text/html

Step 9: End-to-End Testing

1. Deploy both integration flows

2. Trigger the business iFlow using the HTTP endpoint

3. Verify the following:

  • Business iFlow succeeds in Message Monitor
  • Central Error Logger receives the error
  • HTML email is delivered with error details

 

Validation Using Another Business Integration Flow

The same centralized error handling was validated with another real-world integration flow named Invoice_Batch_Email_Processor_With_ProcessDirect.

  • Multiple processing steps
  • Routers based on message severity
  • Multiple email notifications
  • Groovy scripts and content modifiers

 

Key Observations

  • Exception Subprocess captured runtime errors successfully
  • The same central error logger iFlow was reused
  • No changes were required in the logger
  • HTML email alerts were generated correctly

 

This confirms the solution works for both simple and complex integration flows.

Benefits of This Approach

  • Centralized error handling
  • Reusable across multiple iFlows
  • Clean separation of business logic and error handling
  • Faster troubleshooting using Message ID and Correlation ID
  • Professional HTML email notifications
  • Proven in real-world scenarios

Conclusion

By implementing centralized error handling using Exception Subprocess and ProcessDirect adapters, SAP Cloud Integration landscapes become easier to maintain, monitor, and support. This approach reduces duplication, improves observability, and scales efficiently across integration designs.

This solution was implemented and tested in SAP Integration Suite, and all screenshots are from real execution to ensure accuracy and practical relevance.

entry.title: 🚀 SAP Mini Inside Track – Chennai | Coming April 2026
entry.content:
We are excited to announce that the SAP Chennai Community is gearing up to host an upcoming SAP Mini Inside Track (SIT) in April 2026! 🎉

This community‑driven event will bring together SAP professionals, consultants, developers, architects, students, and enthusiasts from across Chennai and nearby regions to collaborate, learn, and share knowledge.

📍 Date & Venue:
Details will be announced very soon — stay tuned!

🌟 What to Expect at SAP Mini Inside Track – Chennai

✅ Cutting‑Edge SAP Topics
Explore insightful sessions covering the latest in SAP technologies, innovations, and real‑world experiences — including S/4HANA, BTP, integrations, cloud, AI, and more.

✅ Knowledge Sharing by the Community
Learn from local SAP experts and practitioners across various organizations who will share practical use cases, lessons learned, and best practices.

✅ Networking & Collaboration
Connect with fellow SAP professionals, exchange ideas, expand your network, and be part of meaningful conversations within the SAP ecosystem.

✅ Open Community Platform
Bring your thoughts, questions, and experiences — this is a collaborative space built by the community, for the community.

🔔 Stay Connected

More information on event agenda, speakers, registration, date, and venue will be shared shortly through this page.

Follow the SAP Chennai Community Page and keep an eye out for updates — exciting times ahead! 🙌

Let’s come together to learn, connect, and grow the SAP ecosystem in Chennai.

#SAPMiniInsideTrack #SAPChennai #SAPCommunity #SAPEvents
#InsideTrack #SAPLearning #SAPNetworking #ChennaiTech #SAPProfessionals

 

Just say the word 👍

Regards

Prasath

entry.title: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the S/4HANA Public Edition (Pt D: Improvements)
entry.content:
Introduction

Now Artificial Intelligence (AI) is on every professional’s mind. Everybody wants to be on the bandwagon.  I plan to write a series of blogs on this topic under a big umbrella “My AI Experience Sharing”.  This is the first mini-series: setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. 

I purposely delayed my action on setting up a Joule to wait for the dust settling down during the Early Adopter phase. The intention paid off but still won’t give me a free pass 😉. There are five credible references available as listed in the Reference section, but none of them give you a good explanation on Why, especially on What in a critical step with a readable picture when you desperately need a hint.  To help all the people like me, this blog will focus on explaining those steps, what to enter, and possible mistakes to avoid. 

This week, I re-did the Joule Setup on a Parallel Line of the SAP S/4HANA ERP Public Edition, aka Public Cloud. I noticed a few changes and would like to outline here for your reference:

You can install Joule on your Parallel Line

Several weeks ago, I met one of our customers at the Customer Success Day in NYC. After listening to his rational behind requiring Joule on the Parallel Line, I promised to do a test. I did just that this week on a Parallel Line. It worked.

No need to NavigationService

I heard there is an improvement in the Setup process, i.e., no more need to manually create the NavigationService destination (outlined in Step B.2.4 of my blog My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the S/4HANA Public Edition (Pt B:Joule Installation). If you continue to add this destination, it won’t hurt because the system is no longer seeking the information stored there. I will remove this step in my blog.

Joule Booster is Faster

The Joule Booster is noticeably faster.  In my case, it is about 15 minutes to complete the running.

SAP Work Zone Content Provider

When I provision the user and group information from the Public Cloud to the Work Zone via IPS, I got an error: The provided providerId is illegal: "status":400,"scimType":"invalidValue".  There is even an KBA addressing this problem 3505749 - The provided providerId is illegal: "status":400,"scimType":"invalidValue".  It turns out not because of my ProviderID is bad, but I only copied part of the ProviderID 6d5e30cb-4 from sap.start.6d5e30cb-4.  I learned that from creating my own ProviderID.  The entire string is the ProviderID, not part of it.  The details count.

References

  1. Blog 1: Joule – End-to-End Setup Guide (for all Line of Business) Unified Approach
  2. Blog 2: Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - Setup Guide
  3. Discovery Center 1: Establish a Unified Joule Instance
  4. Discovery Center 2: Activate Joule with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  5. SAP Help: Joule onboarding guide
  6. My Blog: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Part A Prepara...
  7. My Blog: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Part B Joule I...
  8. My Blog: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Part C Joule D...
  9. My Blog: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Part D Improvements)

entry.title: Inside SAP Build at SAP Sapphire 2026: What to Expect from AI Agents and Agentic Development
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire is almost here, and SAP Build at Sapphire is where customers and partners will be able to see how to easily create and deploy AI agents starting with simple language descriptions.  

For anyone tracking where AI agents and agentic development are headed in the enterprise, SAP Build will offer a chance to see it up close. Attendees will be able to experience how SAP Build helps teams build AI agents across SAP. This includes hands-on experience, live demos, sessions, and direct access to the teams building these capabilities. 

This year, the focus is on making agentic development real - moving from intent to working solution through a guided, end-to-end experience. 

 The hands-on experience: Build an agent with Joule Studio 

The biggest draw for SAP Build at Sapphire will be the hands-on experience on the show floor. 

Attendees can sit down at an open station and work through guided scenarios in a live system – building agents with Joule Studio, generating app logic from intent, and integrating with SAP data and workflows. This is a practical view at how agentic development works from start to deployment. 

This experience builds on the popular SAP Build Developer Garage at TechEd, which became a go-to stop for developers who wanted to get hands-on and see SAP Build in action. At Sapphire, we are bringing that energy back and making it bigger for a broader audience. 

Space will be limited, and access will be first-come, first-serve, so this is one stop worth making early. 

The sessions 

SAP Build sessions at Sapphire will focus on how teams are building AI agents, clean-core extensions and automation workflows. You’ll see how agentic development is evolving - from defining intent to generating and deploying working solutions - and how Joule is integrated across the development lifecycle. 

For anyone trying to separate AI hype from AI execution, these sessions are worth prioritizing. 

The booth 

The SAP Build and Agentic AI booths are where live walk-throughs and real conversations happen. 

Bring your own use cases, connect directly with SAP experts, and validate how SAP Build fits into your architecture. Whether you are building your first extension or scaling AI-driven workflows, the booth’s are designed for both strategic and practical discussions.  

Expect demos, conversations, and a chance to get closer to what SAP Build is showing at Sapphire. 

 

Why stop by SAP Build at SAP Sapphire 

AI agents are a major focus at Sapphire but SAP Build is where you can see how they are actually built and deployed in SAP environments. 

Get hands-on. Talk to the experts. See how agentic development works in practice. 

Plan your SAP Build stop at SAP Sapphire: 

  • Register for SAP Sapphire 
  • Browse SAP Build sessions 
  • Find the SAP Build booth (see the map onsite)

entry.title: SAP Business AI in Public Sector - Partner live expert session
entry.content:
Public sector organizations are under pressure to do more with fewer resources, higher expectations, and constant change.  The opportunity?  AI that actually delivers impact.

In our upcoming SAP Business AI in Public Sector – Partner live expert session, we’ll walk through how you can help customers become more efficient, more responsive, and honestly, more future-ready.

We’ll start with a clear snapshot of the public sector landscape, what’s shifting, what matters.  Then we’ll go deeper into real AI use cases, plus how to position and sell these solutions effectively (because knowing what to sell is only half the story).

There’s also a live demo on SDE 2.0, always a favorite and time for your questions.

If you’re working with public sector clients, this session can sharpen your approach and open new conversations.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013345.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #PublicSector #BusinessAI #DigitalTransformation #GovTech

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite automatic deployments using Ansible
entry.content:
Introduction 

Ansible was included in the architecture with the sole purpose of automating repetitive deployment tasks, reducing the effort, the time spent, restricting the roles/accesses in the the productive tenant, and most important, human errors. By creating only once a file with instructions, Ansible will be able to go through each SAP environment, dynamically execute the steps mentioned, configure parameters, create credentials, deploy artifacts, etc. 

When transporting artifact changes from DEV up to PROD, we, as developers, might need to configure manually, for each system, some of the following: 

  • Users Credential Creation  
  • Updating Known Hosts 
  • Service Keys in SAP Business Technology Platform 
  • Updating external parameters  
  • Import certificates 
  • Deploying integration artifacts  
  • Adding JDBC Material 
  • Configuring Trading Partner Management / API Management 
  • Other 

By using this automated approach, the developers will set up a list of instructions only once, and they will be able to execute these steps as many times as needed.  

Architecture Overview 

Picture 1 - Architecture Overview

 

ServiceNow and Github as source of truth 

For the sake of observability and audit, we wanted to have as much control as possible and be able to see who did what, when, and how. 

With this in mind, we decided that ServiceNow Change Requests together with Github, will be our sources of truth. 

 

  1. Each ServiceNow CHG will contain the Ansible instructions file. 
  1. Each ServiceNow CHG will contain the Jenkins build log. 
  1. Github will hold all the generated Ansible playbooks, failed and successful instructions. 
  1. Github will hold all the sensitive data ( certificates, credentials, etc. ), encrypted using ansible-vault. 

How it works?  

Ansible will start right after the transport stage is done, so the latest artifact version is present in the target environment. 

It will look for a special instructions file in ServiceNow CHGs, and in case it finds it, it starts the process. This instruction file is YAML format, something that can be easily understood by humans, and at the same time by programs. Ansible goes through all steps mentioned in the file and executes them one by one. 

Picture 2 - Sample Ansible Task

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Each step has a list of parameters and a reference to something called a 'task'.  

For us, tasks are API calls, like we do in Postman – set the url, set the body, headers, authentication, etc. 

For Ansible, tasks are logic blocks which contain full details about the API call, can receive a list of parameters to iterate over, can validate response codes, can extract values from the response body, etc. 

Wait. We don't use the same parameters in all environments. 

Sometimes we do, sometimes we don't. For scenarios where the parameters are the same, you can just import and start the pipeline, and the job is done. 

For scenarios where the parameters are different (the usual cases):  

  • Import the yaml, where the whole list of steps is ALREADY defined 
  • Modify the different parameters (Change '100' to '200' for sap-client f.e. ) 
  • Start the pipeline 
  • Done 

Ansible will take care of the actions done on the SAP Integration Suite tenants, it will create security materials, it will save everything in Github, it will log everything in Jenkins, it will validate what you filled in the steps. No more typos, no more errors, no more forgetting to add a certificate, a ssh key, forgot to deploy, etc. 

I am a developer, how do I use it? 

The first step would be to go into the template generator page and check all the needed configuration steps. 

Picture 3 - Template Page

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

After all steps are configured as per the needs of the ServiceNow Change Request, the developer will just review the generated template and push it into the  

Change Request. The template will be automatically pushed into the ServiceNow Change Request, as an attachment, consolidating traceability. 

Picture 4 - Template preview

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

After that, using Jenkins, he can trigger a pipeline to start Ansible and view the logs.  

Picture 5 - SNOW Attachments

 

 

 

 

 

 

Below is a sample Jenkins build log, in which you will see how Ansible is triggered to get the last modified date of a package and create a service key in our SAP Business Technology Platform subaccount.

[Pipeline] echo 
EXECUTING ANSIBLE PLAYBOOK 
[Pipeline] echo 
Playbook: implementation_steps_CHG047545_preprod.yml 
[Pipeline] echo 
Environment: preprod 
[Pipeline] echo 
Ansible Host: localhost:2345 
[Pipeline] echo 
Triggered By: [Admin] 
[Pipeline] echo 
Start Time: 2026-02-12 14:10:24 
[Pipeline] echo 
============================================================ 
[Pipeline] httpRequest 
HttpMethod: POST 
URL: http://localhost:2345/run-playbook/implementation_steps_CHG047545_preprod.yml 
Content-Type: application/json 
Sending request to url: http://localhost:2345/run-playbook/implementation_steps_CHG047545_preprod.yml 
Response Code: HTTP/1.1 200 OK 
Response:  
[WARNING]: Found variable using reserved name: environment 
 
PLAY [Build #22 -- Playbook for CHG047545_preprod] ***************************** 
 
TASK [Task 0 - Initialize global env variable] ********************************* 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [DEBUG - Check environment value] ***************************************** 
ok: [localhost] => { 
    "msg": "The value of environment is 'preprod' and global_env_variable is 'preprod'" 
} 
 
TASK [Task 1 - Initialize global aggregator] *********************************** 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [Task 2 - Execute Authentication] ***************************************** 
 
TASK [0.1 - Get OAuth2 Access Token] ******************************************* 
 
TASK [tools : Step 1 - POST Call for fetching OAuth2 Access Token.] ************ 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [tools : Step 2 - Extracting access_token from the json response.] ******** 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [0.2 - Get CPI API X-CSRF-Token] ****************************************** 
 
TASK [tools : Step 1 - GET Call for fetching X-CSRF-Token.] ******************** 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [tools : Step 2 - Extracting X-CSRF-Token from json response.] ************ 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [auth_helper : 0.3 - Debug current tokens] ******************************** 
skipping: [localhost]

}

    "msg": "Service Instance found successfully for SK-ProcessIntegrationRuntime" 
} 
 
TASK [cf-api : Task 4 - Return message error if status code is not 200 or 202] *** 
skipping: [localhost] => (item=SK-ProcessIntegrationRuntime)

    "msg": "OAuth token retrieval: OK"

    "msg": "Service Key created successfully" 
} 
 
TASK [cf-api : Task 4 - Return message error if status code is not 200 or 202] *** 
skipping: [localhost] 
 
PLAY RECAP ********************************************************************* 
localhost                  : ok=22   changed=0    unreachable=0    failed=0    skipped=5    rescued=0    ignored=0    
 
 
============================================================ 
EXECUTION SUMMARY: 
Playbook: implementation_steps_CHG047545_preprod.yml 
Environment: preprod 
Duration: 7343ms 
Exit Code: 0 
Status: SUCCESS 
============================================================ 
 
Success code from [200‥200] 
[Pipeline] echo 
 
============================================================ 
[Pipeline] echo 
EXECUTION COMPLETED

Reusability 

Picture 6 - Reusability

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

If the developer wants to continue to the next environment, it can just import the previously generated template on the UI template-generator page, change the environment (replace 'test' with 'preprod' in a dropdown) and push the template again. 

Available actions 

We have converted the whole API spec of SAP Integration Suite into dynamic, modular Ansible tasks. Basically anything that is available on SAP Business Accelerator Hub, can be triggered using Ansible.  

 

Picture 7 - Available Tasks

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Security and encryption 

In the instructions file, visible in the ServiceNow attachments and Github storage, we will only see references, no plain text credentials, certificates, API tokens, etc. 

All these references are being used to extract the actual data from a CHG-specific vars file, along with the global vars credentials shared across all ServiceNow Change Request. 

These vars files are using a standard functionality of Ansible, called Ansible Vault. 

Ansible Vault relies on the following cryptographic components: 

  • Cipher: AES-256 in CTR (Counter) mode. 
  • Key Derivation: It uses PBKDF2 (Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2) to turn our plain-text passwords into a high-entropy encryption key. 
  • Hashing: HMAC-SHA256.  
  • Salt: A random salt is generated for every encryption operation, ensuring that the same password and the same plain-text result in a different cipher text every time. 

For example, if we want to deploy a JDBC material  

Picture 9 - Add JDBC

 

 

 

 

 

We will use the CPISNOW_PREPROD reference in the instruction file. This address, along with its details, will be added automatically in that CHGs vars file  

Picture 10 - Vars file

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Above you can see how the vars file looks decrypted. It is a dummy JDBC server, in which all the required fields to deploy the JDBC material are filled. By using a vault pass file, Ansible will decrypt, extract and encrypt back all the files whenever they are needed. Besides that, everything is secured and maintained correctly. 

Auditing & Traceability 

Traceability

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

By saving these recipes as code, they can be subject to an automatic/manual code review process, they can be traced to whoever triggered the pipelines and they're auditable. At any given moment, we have access to the entire workflow – we can see the configured steps, we can see the parameters that were added, we can see the Git commit logs, we can see timestamps, authors, etc. 

Conclusions 

Using automated deployments, the error rate would significantly decrease, since the developer will not go manually and repeat the steps on each environment. The only place where it would be able to make any sort of mistakes would be when generating the template, but even so, the html form contains a lot of validations, and also the dropdowns are dynamically generated with actual data from the system, so it would minimize the error rate. 

Including Github and Jenkins, everything is logged, everything is tracked, everything can be reverted and, in the end, the developers would not even need edit roles in PREPROD/PROD, because every configuration will be done through the technical user. 

 

 

If you have any questions, feel free to reply here, or even go to the dedicated Q&A Section for SAP Integration Suite.

Thank you for your time !

entry.title: What’s new in Mobile development kit client 26.3
entry.content:
Dear Community,

I'm happy to announce that a new release of the Mobile Development Kit (MDK) is now available for all SAP Build and Mobile Services customers. You can download it from the SAP Software Center, and it will soon be available on the community download page.

The SAP Mobile Services Client has been updated to version 26.3.0 on the Apple App Store and the Google Play Store.

SAP MDK enables developers and technical business users to build cross-platform mobile applications. It allows you to build your application once, in an integrated development environment (SAP Business Application Studio / Visual Studio Code) and run it natively on Mobile (iOS & Android).  

Below UI enhancements are available only on iOS and Android platforms:

  • Support Styling of Key Value Item Control
  • Contact Cell Control Enhancements
  • Support In-App Camera in FormCell Attachment Control (Android Only)
  • New ActionBar Caption Size Options
  • Support Required Field Indicator for Segmented FormCell Control
  • Support Uploading Offline Store to Mobile Services
  • Onboarding via MDM App Config (Managed Configuration)

Support Styling of Key Value Item Control

MDK now supports styling Key Value item control. A new object property Styles has been introduced with following sub-properties:

  • KeyValueItem: supports only background color to style entire item.
  • KeyName: supports to style key name.
  • Value: supports to style a value property.
//excerpt of an MDK page
"KeyAndValues": [{
        "Value": "{City}",
        "_Type": "KeyValue.Type.Item",
        "_Name": "KeyValue0",
        "KeyName": "City",
        "Visible": true,
        "OnPress": "/MDK263/Actions/UpdateEntitySuccessMessage.action",
        "LinkColor": "#0000FF",
        "Styles": {
            "KeyValueItem": "KeyValueItemBackground",
            "KeyName": "KeyNameStyle",
            "Value": "KeyValueStyle"
        }
    },

//excerpt of a .less file 
.KeyValueItemBackground {
    background-color: #FFA500;
}
.KeyNameStyle {
    font-color: #ADD8E6;
    text-decoration: underline;
}
.KeyValueStyle {
    font-color: green;
}

Note:  : Value  style will take priority over LinkColor, regardless of whether OnPress is defined or not.

 

Key Value Styling on iOS and Andrid devices

 For more information, refer to the Key Value Item documentation.

Contact Cell Control Enhancements

  • Support for Initials: The ContactCell component now supports displaying user initials, providing a more personalized and recognizable visual experience when profile pictures are unavailable. This enhancement makes it easier to scan and identify contacts by replacing generic placeholder icons with meaningful initials. Three new properties have been introduced to support this feature:
    • DetailImageText: A string property that displays text in the image container when no DetailImage is present. It supports a maximum of 2–4 characters, allowing you to manually specify custom text such as initials.
    • DetailImageIsCircular: A boolean property with a default value of true that controls the shape of the image container. When set to true, the image is displayed in a circular shape; when set to false, it is displayed in a rounded rectangular shape.
    • UseHeadlineForDetailImage: A boolean property with a default value of false. When set to true and no DetailImage is provided, the control automatically extracts and displays the first two initials from the Headline property in the image container. This property takes precedence over DetailImageText.
  • Support for BadgeImage Property:  A new property, BadgeImage, is now available on ContactCell. This property allows you to attach an additional indicator icon or image to the existing DetailImage or DetailImageText for each contact. For example, it can be used to highlight and identify primary contacts in a contact list. Additionally, the existing Styles property for ContactCellItem now supports a new subproperty named BadgeImage, which enables CSS styling for the indicator icon or image defined in the BadgeImage property.
//excerpt of .page
{
    "ContactCell": {
        "_Name": "ContactCellItem6",
        "BadgeImage": "sap-icon://bell",
        "DetailImageText": "SB",
        "DetailImageIsCircular": true,
        "Headline": "Sue Bay",
        "Subheadline": "Antioch, Illinois",
        "UseHeadlineForDetailImage": false,
        "Visible": true,
        "ActivityItems": [{
                "_Name": "ContactCellItem4ActivityItems0",
                "ActivityType": "Phone"
            }
        ],
        "Styles": {
            "DetailImage": "DetailImage",
            "BadgeImage": "BadgeImage"
        }
    }
},

//excerpt of Styles.less
.BadgeImage {
    color: #068831;
    background-color: #ffae00;
}
.DetailImage {
    color: #AD00FF;
    background-color: #ADD8E6;
}

Contact Cell Control on iOS and Android devices

For more information, refer to the Contact Cell documentation.

Support In-App Camera in FormCell Attachment Control (Android Only)

We have received feedback from customers about the MDK app restarting after taking a photo using the Attachment control, especially when the app is running on Android devices with lower RAM. This may be due to memory pressure on the device, which can cause the OS to kill the MDK app running on background after launching the camera.

To address this problem, we are providing support for using an in-app camera option. This option consumes less memory, and the camera opens directly inside the MDK app instead of switching to a separate camera application.

  • A new UseInAppCamera property is available in the Application.app file under the Settings object. It determines whether the in-app camera or the device's default camera will be used for the AttachmentFormCell control’s “Take Photo” option.
    • When set to true, the in-app camera will be used instead of launching the device’s native camera application.
//excerpt of Application.app
{
    "MainPage": "/MDK263/Pages/Main.page",
    "OnLaunch": [
        "/MDK263/Rules/Service/Initialize.js"
    ],
    "Settings": {
        "UseInAppCamera": true
    },
    "_Name": "MDK263"
}

In addition, we are introducing two new APIs to manage the in-app camera settings programmatically:

  • getUseInAppCamera 
    • Retrieves the current state of the in-app camera usage flag.
    • Returns the current value determining whether the in-app camera is enabled for FormCell Attachment controls.
  • setUseInAppCamera 
    • Enables or disables the in-app camera for FormCell Attachment controls
    • Changes take effect immediately for new AttachmentFormCell instances
    • For AttachmentFormCell controls that are already rendered on the current page, the new setting will only take effect after calling the control’s reset() API. Calling the reset() API will discard any existing attachments in the control.
export default function ChangeUseInAppCamera(context) {
  let currentValue = context.getUseInAppCamera();
  context.setUseInAppCamera(!currentValue);
  alert('UseInAppCamera set to ' + !currentValue);
  let currentPageProxy = context.getPageProxy();
  if (currentPageProxy) {
    if (currentPageProxy.getName() === 'FCMain') {
      let attachmentControlProxy = context.evaluateTargetPathForAPI('#Page:FCMain/#Control:FormCellAttachment0');
      if (attachmentControlProxy) {
        attachmentControlProxy.reset();
      }
    }
  }
}

New ActionBar Caption Size Options

The ActionBar now supports flexible caption sizing through the new PreferredCaptionSize property, which replaces the existing PrefersLargeCaption property.

  • Enhanced size control: Choose from small, medium, or large caption sizes instead of toggling between only two options.
    • iOS supports small and large caption size
    • Android supports small, medium, and large caption size.

Behavior Notes:        

  • When PreferredCaptionSize is set to "large", any defined Subhead will be ignored.
  • When setting CaptionAlignment (Android only) to “center”, the PreferCaptionSize automatically set to small.  
  • On iOS, large caption mode is not supported on pages with BottomNavigation, Tabs, SideDrawer, FormCellContainer, or FlexibleColumn controls.
  • If both PreferredCaptionSize and the deprecated PrefersLargeCaption property are specified, PreferredCaptionSize takes precedence.
//excerpt of an MDK page 
"ActionBar": {
        "Items": [
            {
                "_Type": "Control.Type.ActionBarItem",
                "_Name": "ActionBarItem0",
                "Caption": "User Menu",
                "OnPress": "/MDK263/Actions/Application/UserMenuPopover.action"
            }
        ],
        "_Name": "ActionBar1",
        "_Type": "Control.Type.ActionBar",
        "Caption": "MDK 26.3",
        "PreferredCaptionSize": "Medium"
    }
}

 

Support Required Field Indicator  for Segmented FormCell Control 

In MDK 25.9 release, we introduced support for required field indicators for some FormCell controls. In this release, we are extending this support to the Segmented FormCell, indicating that the field must be filled out.
Note: This feature does not enforce users to fill out the form cell; it only provides a visual indicator that the field is required.

//Excerpt from a .page
{
    "RequiredIndicator": true,
    "_Type": "Control.Type.FormCell.SegmentedControl",
    "_Name": "PrioritySegmented",
    "Caption": "Priority",
    "ApportionsSegmentWidthsByContent": false,
    "Segments": [
        "Low",
        "Medium",
        "High"
    ]
}

Form Cell Segmented control on iOS and Android devices

 

Support Uploading Offline Store to Mobile Services 

MDK now provides the ability for applications to upload the offline store (Both the main offline database (UDB) and the Request Queue database) to Mobile Services to assist with troubleshooting. This capability helps developers and support teams more effectively investigate and diagnose issues related to the offline store, reducing the time required to identify the root cause of problems reported by business users.
To facilitate this capability, a new MDK action has been introduced: Action.Type.OfflineOData.UploadStore.

{
    "_Type": "Action.Type.OfflineOData.UploadStore",
    "ActionResult": {
        "_Name": "UploadOfflineDB"
    },
    "Service": "/MDK263/Services/SampleServiceV4.service",
    "EncryptionKey": "MDK123", // encryption key used during the upload process
    "Note": "My Test App" // Optional note attached to the uploaded database file
}

In order to use this feature, you will need to first enable offline store upload policy in SAP Mobile Services Admin UI for a given MDK app. Developers can download the uploaded files on their local machine and use ILODATA tool (link to doc) to analyze them.  (link how to use this tool). For more information, see this  documentation.

Onboarding via MDM App Config (Managed Configuration)

MDK now supports onboarding via MDM App Config (Managed Configuration). Company administrators can push onboarding settings directly to managed devices through their MDM solution.

The MDK client retrieves MDK-defined configurations from a reserved root property named MDKSettings within the Managed Configuration. Currently, this root property only accepts the ConnectionSettings object. The supported properties, their functionality, and the required structure for ConnectionSettings remain the same as in the existing implementation.

While the iOS and Android versions differ syntactically due to their file formats, they share the same structural hierarchy. Note that the actual syntax may vary depending on the requirements of the specific MDM solution being used.

  • iOS: MDM solutions for iOS commonly use an XML format for complex configurations.
  • Android: MDM solutions for Android commonly use a JSON format for complex configurations.

Become an SAP MDK Explorer!
Do you have a requirement to build a mobile solution for your enterprise? Take your first steps today by following these tutorials to get started and learn more about the SAP Mobile development kit. We’re excited to see what you build—your feedback and ideas are invaluable!

Submit Improvement Requests
You can submit your improvement request (link) for Mobile development kit to the SAP Customer Influence site, a central place for all product improvement requests. You can browse all improvement requests, submit improvement requests, comment, vote, receive updates, and see who has voted at the SAP Customer Influence site.

entry.title: Terraform in Practice: Setting Up Joule Studio
entry.content:
Introduction 

As Developer Advocates, we run SAP CodeJam events where participants need hands-on access to the Joule Studio environment, typically provided by us during the day of the event. The challenge: a Joule Studio tenant has limits on the number of deployments that can be made, and these limits are reached in a CodeJam setting where 30+ developers are simultaneously testing their skills and agents, pushing deployments etc, so each CodeJam event requires spinning up a brand new tenant from scratch knowing which subscription, instances, plan, role collections, destinations, and credentials to use while creating a new tenant. 

Doing these tasks by hand is a long and tedious process. It takes too many clicks and careful typing, which makes it easy to make a mistake. With CodeJam events happening frequently, this process was becoming a real bottleneck. Terraform seemed like a good choice at this point to perform these steps. Instead of repeating the same manual steps every time, we could automate the entire tenant provisioning process, turning what used to take significant manual effort into a few commands and getting a Joule Studio tenant at the end of the execution which typically takes 20 to 30 mins. 

I had the opportunity to explore Terraform during a hands-on session at SAP Inside Track, Bangalore in June 2025.  After trying it out in the developer hands on area, I could see its value for automating the creation and management of SAP BTP subaccounts. During Devtoberfest 2025, SAP experts on Terraform presented a session titled From Clicks to Code: Automate SAP BTP Setup with Terraform on how to set up a BTP environment with Terraform. While trying to learn more about Terraform, I also came across a learning.sap.com course that is comphrensive. The course is available here and the GitHub repository used in this learning course is available here. There are also some excellent posts in the SAP Community portal for beginners looking to get started with Terraform, the links to those posts can be found in the Additional References section. Rather than repeating what's already well-documented, I'll focus on the key concepts behind the Terraform and share a few things I picked up along the way and provide you a Terraform script to get started with Joule Studio setup.  

What is Terraform? 

Terraform uses HashiCorp Configuration Language for building infrastructure with configuration files. Terraform's configuration language is declarative, meaning that it describes the desired end state for your infrastructure. It also keeps track of your real infrastructure in a state file, which acts as a source of truth for your environment. Terraform uses the state file to determine the changes to make to your infrastructure so that it will match your configuration. Terraform builds a resource graph to determine resource dependencies and creates or modifies non-dependent resources in parallel. This allows Terraform to provision resources efficiently. Terraform plugins called providers lets Terraform interact with cloud platforms and other services via their application programming interfaces (APIs). SAP provides dedicated Terraform providers for BTP, Cloud Identity, Cloud Foundry, and Cloud Connector. The BTP provider functions by wrapping BTP CLI commands, allowing you to convert imperative CLI commands into declarative Terraform resources. See the registry for documentation on how to use them: https://registry.terraform.io/search/providers?namespace=SAP. 

Using Terraform as an Administrator 

There are two ways to run the terraform script to create a Joule Studio environment. We will look at how to run the Terraform scripts on a local machine with Terraform CLI first. The approach mentioned below requires minor changes depending on whether we are running it on a Mac or Windows machine. 

  1. Clone this repository https://github.com/SAP-samples/joule-studio-setup-terraform
  2. Open it in VS Code
  3. Go through the pre-requisites section in the README file 
  4. Install Terraform CLI https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/tutorials/aws-get-started/install-cli 
  5. Set up environment variables by running these commands on your VS Code terminal  

On Mac/Linux 

export TF_VAR_btp_username='your-btp-username' 
export TF_VAR_btp_password='your-btp-user-password'

On Windows (PowerShell) 

$env:TF_VAR_btp_username = 'your-btp-username' 
$env:TF_VAR_btp_password = 'your-btp-user-password'

6. Configure the values for global account subdomain, identity provider domain, subdomain name and region in the terraform.tfvars file

Project in VS Code 7. Go to the root directory and run the commands from your terminal one by one. 

terraform init
terraform apply --auto-approve

The terraform init command installs the SAP BTP and Cloud foundry providers configured in the main.tf file in the repository.  

The terraform apply command installs the entitlements and subscriptions mentioned in the .tf files. 

8. Once all the resources are created, run Joule booster to create formation to complete the Joule studio setup. See the steps here on running the Joule booster. 

9. Navigate to the Instances and Subscriptions page of your new BTP subaccount; ensure the following subscriptions and service instances have been successfully provisioned.

The second approach is explained in the GitHub Actions section. 

GitHub Actions 

Now, the local Terraform CLI approach works perfectly fine and well suited for a development workflow, but what if you would rather not install anything on your machine at all? 

That's where GitHub Actions comes in. GitHub Actions lets you automate anything, triggered either by an event in your repository (like a push or a PR) or kicked off manually straight from the GitHub UI. Your workflows live as YAML files inside the .github/workflows directory of your repository. 

Check out the README of this repository to follow this approach for creating your Joule Studio setup: https://github.com/SAP-samples/joule-studio-setup-terraform 

One thing worth knowing: you can override any terraform.tfvars variables defined in your repository with the corresponding values in the GitHub variables by passing them via the --var flag. You can see how to pass the variables in the terraform.yml file under .github/workflows of the repository. Anything you haven't explicitly overridden will simply fall back to whatever is defined in terraform.tfvars. 

Use this approach when you have a stable version of a script that can be used to create a new tenant with all the resources and dependencies. 

Using Terraform as a Developer 

My recommendation would be to stick to the local Terraform CLI approach while you are actively writing or updating your scripts. The reason is simple: when something fails during development, Terraform lets you fix just the broken parts and apply only those changes. That kind of control is needed when you are iterating quickly. 

Before you start customizing the script to fit your needs, there are also a handful of expressions and constructs worth getting accustomed to.  So, let’s walk through the significant ones. 

Variables 

All the variables used in the script are defined in the variables.tf file with an optional default value if needed. Configurable values such as global account name, sub account region etc can be provided in the terraform.tfvars file. Sensitive information like passwords and secrets should be stored as environment variables in local machines or as GitHub secrets in GitHub. Terraform makes this easy with the TF_VAR_ prefix. Any environment variable with that prefix gets automatically picked up as a Terraform input variable. So, if you set TF_VAR_btp_password in your environment, Terraform maps it directly to var.btp_password in your script. This keeps credentials in GitHub Secrets or Environment variables and passes them securely to Terraform without exposing them in logs or files. 

If a variable has no default value and you haven't provided it through .tfvars or an environment variable, Terraform will prompt you for it in the terminal during plan or apply. It won't silently fail, but it will interrupt your flow, so it's worth getting your variables sorted before you run anything.  

Built -in functions: 

There is a set of built-in functions available that you can use within your scripts from this reference. Some of the functions used in the repository are lower(), replace(), trim(), toset(), contains() etc. When a subaccount is created, the name of the subaccount subdomain needs to confirm certain rules defined by BTP. We could use these built-in functions to sanitize the user input provided in the terraform.tfvars file and create a subaccount subdomain that confirms those naming conventions.  

raw_subdomain = lower("${var.subaccount_name}-${var.subaccount_subdomain}-${var.subaccount_region}") 
subaccount_subdomain = trim (replace (replace (raw_subdomain, "/[^a-z0-9-]/", "-"), "/--+/", "-"), "-")

This code sanitizes and formats a string to create a subdomain by concatenating three variables into lowercase and using regular expressions to replace any invalid special characters or double-hyphens with a single dash.

Meta-argument 

depends_on is a meta-argument to define explicit dependency. For eg: if you want to create a subscription for Build Work Zone but the entitlement for Build Work Zone should exist before the subscription happens. In such cases, the depends_on argument can be used to ensure that the entitlement is created before the subscription. By default, Terraform executes resources in parallel.

local values block  

When you want to define a local variable or derive a value from a variable, you can use locals. For instance, the cf_api_url is derived from subaccount_region variable, so we can define it within local value block and then use them by referencing it as local.cf_api.url  

locals {
  cf_api_url = https://api.cf.${var.subaccount_region}.hana.ondemand.com 
}

Ternary operators 

Ternary operators can be used to apply conditional expressions. 

plan_name = var.environment == "prod" ? "standard" : "free"

If the environment is production, it assigns the value "standard" to plan_name.

What I learned about the Development workflow 

 After working with Terraform, here is what I consider to be the best practices to follow: 

  • Always start with terraform fmt -recursive before committing anything. It ensures your code style remains consistent and identifies basic syntax issues early, preventing them from causing problems later
  • Run terraform validate after every code change. It's quick, doesn't need credentials, and gives you instant feedback on syntax and data type errors 
  • Never skip the terraform plan -out=tfplan even for "small" changes. This step shows you exactly what Terraform is about to create, modify, or destroy. Review the output carefully before moving on
  • Then apply with terraform apply tfplan using the saved plan file. This guarantees you are applying exactly what you reviewed 
  • Avoid --auto-approve flags in production
  • Never commit .tfstate files as state files can contain sensitive data like passwords and secrets. Add this file to .gitignore. Use a secure remote state backend instead 
  • Always commit .terraform.lock.hcl. This locks your provider versions and ensures everyone on the team gets reproducible results

Further extension of the script 

Think of the script as your starting point, it gets you a working Joule Studio setup quickly. Depending on your scenario, you will likely want to extend it.  

For Joule to connect to SAP AI Core (for document grounding), an S3 bucket (for storing documents), an MCP Server (for access to live data) or external OData APIs via BTP destinations, you will need to add those destinations in Terraform. Create a new destination.tf file, add your destination configurations and commit it to the repository and execute the terraform cli commands.

One other thing worth mentioning is Cloud Identity Services. The script assumes your global account already has Cloud Identity Services set up, which is the case for most existing BTP accounts. If that is your case, all you need to do is provide the identity provider domain value in terraform.tfvars and you are good to go. 

However, if your account doesn't have Cloud Identity Services configured yet in the global account, you will need to add a resource for the entitlement and subscription as well. For that, I would point you to the Cloud Identity Services provider documentation, it covers exactly what you need to set that up. 

 Refer to the samples shown here for setting up destination and Cloud Identity Services

  •  https://registry.terraform.io/providers/SAP/btp/latest/docs/resources/subaccount_destination 
  • https://registry.terraform.io/providers/SAP/sap-cloud-identity-services/latest/docs 

 Additional References

  • https://github.com/SAP-samples/btp-terraform-samples 
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/automating-sap-btp-setup-with-the-new-terraform-provider-for-sap-btp/ba-p/13549469 
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/extending-infrastructure-as-code-for-sap-btp-managing-resources-on-sap-btp/ba-p/14062455 
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/easy-way-to-automate-and-configure-the-setup-of-a-btp-account-and-cloud/ba-p/13677419

entry.title: [UPDATE] BTP Trial Update: Kyma Runtime Availability
entry.content:
Dear SAP BTP trial community,

We always aimed to give users the most freedom and flexibility in the SAP BTP trial to ensure that everyone can test and explore our software or use it for learning purposes. For the next weeks, the SAP BTP trial will not be able to deliver this experience for new SAP BTP trial accounts as we have to prioritize the security and reliability of our SAP BTP trial system. We are experiencing an unusually high amount of misuse limiting the availability for all of our users. 

Therefore, we decided to temporarily reduce the offering for the SAP BTP Kyma runtime for new BTP Trial Accounts. You will still be able to build applications and preview them, but deployment in the SAP BTP Kyma runtime will not be possible for the next weeks. 

How can you still explore SAP BTP? 

  • All other services will remain available and can be explored on the SAP Discovery Center
  • As a partner: Refer to our newly released free TDD licenses
  • As a customer: Leverage the free tier offering as part of Pay-As-You-Go for SAP BTP available in the SAP Store

All updates will be posted in the SAP Community. 

 

See you soon, 

SAP BTP trial product team

entry.title: AI and UX sessions at SAP Sapphire 2026 that I’m most excited about
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire is where SAP makes its big announcements about its vision for the future of ERP and business applications. This includes showing new products, presenting customers who have transformed their business using SAP’s solutions, and highlighting new technologies that will shape the future.

For 2026, AI will continue to dominate the event. AI will change the way that people consume enterprise software through chat-based interfaces and screens that generate on-the-fly based on what you want to do. Eventually.

In this post, I’ll share the sessions at Sapphire and ASUG Annual Conference that I’m most excited about. For the full menu, you can browse the session catalog. My preferences are, of course, shaped by my role on the Cloud ERP UX Foundation team. I’ll organize by date so you can plan your agenda. You can also use the browser to search by topic or speaker if that makes it easier for you. Some are even available on-demand. Let’s go!

Monday, May 11, 2026

Day / Time / Session ID

Session title and description

Speakers

Monday
10am – 5pm
ASUG102

Unlocking Business Value with AI: Strategy, Security, and the Road Ahead

Gain a comprehensive and practical overview of SAP Business AI. This workshop will examine core strategies, security frameworks, and future trends while exploring real-world customer stories. The session will conclude with an expert panel and a hands-on AI road mapping session.

Many experts from SAP

Monday
2:00pm – 2:20pm
ERP2143

The future of enterprise: Innovations in SAP Cloud ERP Private

Discover how SAP Cloud ERP Private solutions enable the next era of enterprise transformation. This introductory session explores how cutting-edge artificial intelligence, trusted data, and integrated applications work in harmony, creating smarter, faster, and more resilient businesses within the security and flexibility of your private cloud.

Nicole Smythe, SAP

Vinay V, SAP

Monday
2:30pm – 2:50pm
CXP2149

Legacy to legendary: The AI-enhanced path

Explore the strategic road map for moving from legacy on-premises systems to a cloud-native, AI-enhanced architecture. Discover how a clean core approach helps reduce technical debt, accelerate innovation, and position your organization to use AI for greater efficiency and agility.

 

Monday
3:30pm – 3:50pm
PUX1349

Innovations and value that SAP Business AI offers

Learn how you can find the best AI features for your use cases, and understand the business value and associated cost with our self-service tools.

Pascal Mauge, SAP

 

Tuesday, May 12, 2026

Day / Time / Session ID

Session title and description

Speakers

Tuesday
10:30 am - 11:15 am PUX1228

Creating better user experiences with AI-assisted SAP Fiori apps | PUX1228

New AI features in SAP Fiori apps boost user productivity and speed onboarding. Starting with the “My Home” page and drilling down into typical SAP Fiori apps, see how AI makes it easier to find information, enter data, automate tasks, and make informed business decisions. Explore how conversational interactions make SAP solutions more intuitive.

 

Peter Spielvogel, SAP

Tuesday
11:00 am - 11:20 am PUX1346

Great features don’t guarantee success: Experience does

Explore SAP Business Suite in the cloud to discover how you can shape the experience of the product for stellar adoption success.

Daniel Becker, SAP

Tuesday
11:30 am - 12:15 pm FOU1861

ASUG Community Conversation: Artificial intelligence

Explore platform strategy, embedded and generative AI use cases, integration patterns, and road map priorities with experts in AI and SAP Business Technology Platform. Discuss architecture, data readiness, and clean core alignment while exchanging lessons learned with peers advancing AI innovation.

John Astill, SAP

Tuesday
11:30 am - 12:15 pm ERP1622

SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA: What’s new, why change, and why now?

 Learn from more than 2,400 cloud ERP projects as we navigate the shift from SAP ERP Central Component (SAP ECC) to SAP S/4HANA. Compare greenfield, brownfield, and selective data transitions to find the right path. Discover the key differences in SAP S/4HANA, the value of moving now, and how to accelerate AI adoption with modern technology.

 

Esther Vrieze, SAP

Bert Schulze, SAP

Tuesday
11:30 am - 12:15 pm BTM2345

Why ERP transformations fail: The change management gap

Organizations expect operational efficiency and digital capabilities from their SAP solution transformations, yet some ERP programs fail to realize full value. The missing link is often adoption. See how integrating organizational change management early in the SAP lifecycle reduces resistance, accelerates adoption, and improves business outcomes.

 

Kara Beck, Moog Inc.

Tuesday
1:00 pm - 1:45 pm ERP1864

ASUG Community Conversation: Cloud ERP and SAP S/4HANA

In this interactive ASUG Community Conversation for professionals in cloud ERP and SAP S/4HANA, explore strategies across migration, operation, and innovation—featuring our road maps, clean core and AI insights, and customer lessons learned. Exchange practical guidance with peers and shape future discussions through open dialogue.

Peter Spielvogel, SAP

Nicole Smythe, SAP

Tuesday
1:00 pm - 1:20 pm JOU2645

The future of enterprise applications and AI experiences

Unlock real value from agentic AI with insights from SAP experts and peers. Explore what transformation means today and how you can scale AI across the business, overcome common challenges, govern risk, and build an AI-first enterprise that drives measurable top- and bottom-line impact.

 

Tuesday
2:00 pm - 2:45 pm ERP1178

2026 and beyond: Discover the latest innovations in SAP Cloud ERP Private 

Running your ERP in the cloud allows you to benefit from innovations at your own pace. Explore recent AI and other functional developments planned for the next releases of SAP Cloud ERP Private solutions to help you prepare for adoption, build your road map, and accelerate time to value.

Maura Hameroff, SAP

Rudolf Hois, SAP

Tuesday
3:00 pm - 3:45 pm FOU1559

Build enterprise-grade pro-code agents on SAP Business Technology Platform

Unlock the power of autonomous agents with enterprise-grade guarantees. Get technical tips on building pro-code agents on SAP Business Technology Platform that balance innovation with responsibility. Learn to use the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for smooth tool integration while supporting security, ethical AI behavior, and regulatory compliance.

Evgenii Skrebtcov, SAP

Tim Back, SAP

Tuesday
3:00 pm - 3:45 pm ERP1834

Extend SAP S/4HANA Cloud while keeping a clean core | ERP1834

Extend the capabilities of SAP S/4HANA Cloud to address your business needs. Use AI to generate projects in the ABAP programming language and SAP Cloud Application Programming Model from your business requirements. Build SAP Fiori apps based on your written or graphical description, and use natural language filtering to find information in them.

Katja Zoch, SAP

Tuesday
3:30 pm - 3:50 pm JOU2646

AI as the new UI

The interface is disappearing, and that's the point. As AI becomes the primary way people interact with enterprise software, the question is no longer how to design better screens but how to deliver better outcomes. Intent replaces navigation, conversation replaces configuration, and the most powerful user experience is the one you barely notice.

 

Tuesday
4:00 pm - 4:20 pm PUX1192

SAP's user experience strategy for an AI-powered web and mobile future

Get an overview of how SAP is helping business users get their work done more easily and with better outcomes: using the Joule solution and embedded AI to take UX to the next level, providing a consistent and integrated suite experience, and leveraging our user research framework to meet users' needs. Also see how you can create great UX yourself.

Arin Bhowmick, SAP

 

Wednesday, May 13, 2026

Day / Time / Session ID

Session title and description

Speakers

Wednesday
10:30 am – 11:15 am SAP1623

SAP ECC to SAP Business Suite: Migration strategies and cloud insights

Master the direct transition from SAP ERP Central Component to SAP Business Suite using proven best practices. Compare public, private, and on-premises deployments to define key decision drivers. Learn to navigate annual releases, leverage essential migration tools, and identify the evolving skills needed for a successful, seamless cloud journey.

Esther Vrieze, SAP

Bert Schultze, SAP

Wednesday
10:30 am – 11:15 am PUX1819

SAP Fiori in action: How modern UX drives efficiency in cloud ERP

Get an overview of how the SAP Fiori user experience (UX) helps users get their work done more easily and efficiently. See how we are using AI today in applications and with the Joule solution. Understand our road map for revolutionizing UX, how AI changes the idea of what an application is, and what all this means for you.

Thomas Reiss, SAP

Wednesday
11:30 am – 11:50 am SUS1523

AI that helps you operate your business

As sustainability shifts from reporting to real business decisions, it becomes a control and execution challenge. Explore how you can replace manual processes with AI for trusted, scalable insights and decision support, delivering accountability for operational and financial decision-making.

Sophia Mendelsohn, SAP

Wednesday
11:30 am – 12:15 pm PUX1195

Creating AI-powered UX for your own applications with SAP Design System

Learn how to create AI-powered UX for your applications by using SAP Design System (SAP Fiori UX for web and mobile apps) with its powerful set of reusable components, patterns, and floorplans. See how building applications is made easier with our AI-enabled UI technologies for web and mobile apps.

Sophia Levens, SAP

Frederic Berg, SAP

Wednesday
11:30 am – 12:15 pm ERP2238

Western Sugar’s success with SAP Cloud ERP

Five years after go-live, Western Sugar Cooperative calls SAP Cloud ERP applications “wonderful.” Hear about why it chose public cloud; the value of clean core, best practices, strong configuration and integration, and continuous improvement; its smooth move from a two- to three-system landscape; and readiness for AI-enabled innovation.

 

Wednesday
11:30 am – 12:15 pm FOU2337

When AI becomes the interface to the enterprise

For decades, SAP helped shape enterprise software based on screens and transactions. Today, AI is evolving how we interact with systems. Explore how SAP leads this shift—uniting applications, data, and AI—and what this next phase of transformation means for organizations turning AI into a real competitive advantage.

Florian Heretsch, SAP

Wednesday
1:00 pm – 1:45 pm FOU1557

Getting started with SAP Build MCPs: Create your first CAP app and process

 Experience the powerful Model Context Protocol (MCP) integration in SAP Build solutions. Produce your first SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) application and SAP Fiori elements UI, while orchestrating workflows. See how AI-enhanced context improves coding efficiency and helps you build scalable, process-rich apps.

 

Tim Back, SAP

Jason Gabor, SAP

Wednesday
1:00 pm – 1:45 pm BTM1232

The AI-augmented architect: Upgrading architecture in the age of AI 

 AI is reshaping enterprise architecture. To provide value, enterprise architectures must embrace AI tools. Discover SAP technology leveraging AI for enhanced visibility and coordination. Through real-world examples, see how Joule and agentic AI increase information velocity and augment architect capabilities to thrive in this evolving landscape.

Martin Mysyk, SAP

Wednesday
2:00 pm – 2:20 pm PUX1210

AI and the future of user experience in SAP software

Prepare yourself for the upcoming revolution in user experience by understanding how we are harnessing AI to help users achieve better outcomes. Discover how AI is changing the idea of what an application is—and what this means for you. Learn about our AI design patterns and get a glimpse of the future SAP software user experience.

Emil Voutta, SAP

Wednesday
2:00 pm – 2:20 pm JOU2647

The future of enterprise applications and AI experiences

The enterprise application is no longer a system of record but a system of action. As AI moves from assistant to agent, organizations shift from what their software can store to what it can do. Learn why the future belongs to applications that don't just surface information but understand context, anticipate decisions, and act with confidence.

 

Wednesday
3:00 pm – 3:20 pm PUX1194

User experience innovations and road map for SAP Business Suite

Have a look at the latest user experience innovations across our suite of applications, along with exciting web and mobile innovations coming soon. Also check out selected highlights from Ul technologies for creating great UX for your own applications.

Tom Reiss, SAP

I’m excited to hear what we announce in the keynotes and experience the future of enterprise software. AI will transform how we all interact with apps. The future starts now.

Here are some other summaries of Sapphire sessions from other colleagues.

Inside SAP Build at SAP Sapphire 2026: What to Expect from AI Agents and Agentic Development by Mark Wright

See you in Orlando!

For the SAP Cloud ERP UX Foundation team, Peter Spielvogel.
